section.title: Authority, the Tenants, and the Questions
section.sectionRationale: In the temple the leaders challenge Jesus' authority, and He answers with a question about John that traps them, then tells the parable of the wicked tenants who kill the owner's beloved son, naming Himself the rejected stone become the cornerstone. A series of hostile questions follows, on taxes to Caesar, on the resurrection, and on the Christ as David's Lord, and Jesus answers each with such wisdom that they dare ask no more. He closes by warning the crowd against the scribes who devour widows' houses and love display.
section.thesis: Jesus teaches in the temple as the Lord of it, exposing the leaders who reject Him as the murderous tenants and revealing Himself as the rejected cornerstone, the Lord of Caesar and of the living God of resurrection, David's Lord as well as David's son.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the challenge to Jesus' authority; the parable of the wicked tenants and the cornerstone; the question about taxes to Caesar; the Sadducees on the resurrection; and the Christ as David's Lord, with a warning against the scribes.

units.label: The challenge to Jesus' authority
units.note: by what authority are You doing these things

verses.text: One day as Jesus was teaching the people in the temple courts and proclaiming the gospel, the chief priests and scribes, together with the elders, came up to Him.
“Tell us,” they said, “by what {{exousia|authority}} are You doing these things, and who gave You this authority?”
“I will also ask you a question,” Jesus replied. “Tell Me:
John’s baptism, was it from heaven, or from men?”
They deliberated among themselves and said, “If we say, ‘From heaven,’ He will ask, ‘Why did you not believe him?’
But if we say, ‘From men,’ all the people will stone us, for they are convinced that John was a prophet.”
So they answered that they did not know where it was from.
And Jesus replied, “Neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things.”

units.label: The parable of the wicked tenants
units.note: the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone

verses.text: Then He proceeded to tell the people this parable: “A man planted a vineyard, rented it out to some tenants, and went away for a long time.
At harvest time, he sent a servant to the tenants to collect his share of the fruit of the vineyard. But the tenants beat the servant and sent him away empty-handed.
So he sent another servant, but they beat him and treated him shamefully, sending him away empty-handed.
Then he sent a third, but they wounded him and threw him out.
‘What shall I do?’ asked the owner of the vineyard. ‘I will send my beloved son. Perhaps they will respect him.’
But when the tenants saw the son, they discussed it among themselves and said, ‘This is the heir. Let us kill him, and the inheritance will be ours.’
So they threw him out of the vineyard and killed him. What then will the owner of the vineyard do to them?
He will come and kill those tenants and give the vineyard to others.” And when the people heard this, they said, “May such a thing never happen!”
But Jesus looked directly at them and said, “Then what is the meaning of that which is written: ‘The stone the builders rejected has become the {{kephalegonias|cornerstone}}’?
Everyone who falls on this stone will be broken to pieces, but he on whom it falls will be crushed.”

units.label: Paying taxes to Caesar
units.note: give to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's

verses.text: When the scribes and chief priests realized that Jesus had spoken this parable against them, they sought to arrest Him that very hour. But they were afraid of the people.
So they watched Him closely and sent spies who pretended to be sincere. They were hoping to catch Him in His words in order to hand Him over to the rule and authority of the governor.
“Teacher,” they inquired, “we know that You speak and teach correctly. You show no partiality but teach the way of God in accordance with the truth.
Is it lawful for us to pay taxes to Caesar or not?”
But Jesus saw through their duplicity and said to them,
“Show Me a denarius. Whose image and inscription are on it?” “Caesar’s,” they answered.
So Jesus told them, “Give to Caesar what is Caesar’s, and to God what is God’s.”
And they were unable to trap Him in His words before the people. And amazed at His answer, they fell silent.

units.label: The Sadducees and the resurrection
units.note: He is not the God of the dead, but of the living

verses.text: Then some of the Sadducees, who say there is no {{anastasis|resurrection}}, came to question Him.
“Teacher,” they said, “Moses wrote for us that if a man’s brother dies and leaves a wife but no children, the man is to marry his brother’s widow and raise up offspring for him.
Now there were seven brothers. The first one married a wife but died childless.
Then the second
and the third married the widow, and in the same way all seven died, leaving no children.
And last of all, the woman died.
So then, in the resurrection, whose wife will she be? For all seven were married to her.”
Jesus answered, “The sons of this age marry and are given in marriage.
But those who are considered worthy to share in the age to come and in the resurrection from the dead will neither marry nor be given in marriage.
In fact, they can no longer die, because they are like the angels. And since they are sons of the resurrection, they are sons of God.
Even Moses demonstrates that the dead are raised, in the passage about the burning bush. For he calls the Lord ‘the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.’
He is not the God of the dead, but of the living, for to Him all are alive.”
Some of the scribes answered, “Teacher, You have spoken well!”
And they did not dare to question Him any further.

units.label: David's Lord and a warning against the scribes
units.note: David calls Him Lord, so how can He be David's son

verses.text: Then Jesus declared, “How can it be said that the Christ is the Son of David?
For David himself says in the book of Psalms: ‘The Lord said to my Lord, “Sit at My right hand
until I make Your enemies a footstool for Your feet.”’
Thus David calls Him ‘{{kyrios|Lord}}.’ So how can He be David’s son?”
In the hearing of all the people, Jesus said to His disciples,
“Beware of the scribes. They like to walk around in long robes, and they love the greetings in the marketplaces, the chief seats in the synagogues, and the places of honor at banquets.
They defraud widows of their houses, and for a show make lengthy prayers. These men will receive greater condemnation.”

exousia.gloss: Authority, the right and power to act. The leaders demand to know by what authority Jesus teaches and cleanses the temple. He answers with a question about John that exposes their bad faith, for the real issue is whether they will submit to the authority of heaven that stands before them in Him.

kephalegonias.gloss: The head of the corner, the cornerstone. Quoting Psalm 118, Jesus applies to Himself the stone the builders rejected which becomes the chief cornerstone. The builders are the leaders who reject Him; God overturns their verdict and makes the rejected Son the foundation of His new house.

anastasis.gloss: Resurrection, a rising up. The Sadducees deny it, but Jesus proves it from Moses himself: God calls Himself the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob long after their deaths, and He is the God of the living. The resurrection is not a puzzle to be mocked but the promise of a God to whom all His people are alive.

kyrios.gloss: Lord, master. In Psalm 110 David calls the Messiah his Lord, which means the Christ is more than merely David's descendant; He is David's Lord, exalted at God's right hand. Jesus points to His own divine identity, the Son of David who is also the Lord of David.

basic.context: Teaching in the temple, Jesus is confronted by the chief priests, scribes, and elders demanding to know by what authority He acts. He answers with a counter-question about John's baptism that leaves them trapped, refusing to say. Then He tells of a vineyard owner whose tenants beat his servants and finally kill his beloved son to seize the inheritance; the owner will destroy them and give the vineyard to others. Jesus names Himself the rejected stone that becomes the cornerstone. His enemies send spies to trap Him on paying taxes to Caesar, and He answers, give to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's. The Sadducees pose a riddle to mock the resurrection, and He proves it from Moses: God is the God of the living. Then He asks how the Christ can be both David's son and David's Lord, and warns the crowd against the proud, widow-devouring scribes.
basic.hermeneutics: The scene is a series of confrontations in which the leaders try to trap Jesus and fail, revealing His authority and their unbelief. The parable is the interpretive center: they are the tenants who reject the Son. The questions that follow show Jesus as Lord over Caesar, over death, and over David himself, silencing every challenge.
basic.originalLanguages: The leaders demand His exousia, authority, but will not own the authority of heaven before them. Jesus names Himself the kephale gonias, the cornerstone the builders rejected. He proves the anastasis, resurrection, from Moses, since God is God of the living. And He shows that the Christ is David's kyrios, Lord, not merely his son.
basic.typology: The vineyard is Israel from Isaiah's song, its tenants the faithless leaders, its rejected son the Messiah. The rejected stone become cornerstone, from Psalm 118, is fulfilled in Christ crucified and raised. The God of the burning bush, God of the living patriarchs, guarantees the resurrection Jesus embodies.
basic.christ: Jesus is the beloved Son sent last to the vineyard and killed by the tenants, the rejected stone God makes the cornerstone. He is Lord over Caesar's realm and God's, the One who proves and will enact the resurrection, and David's Lord seated at God's right hand, the Christ who is more than a son of David.
basic.god: God is the patient owner who sends servant after servant and at last His beloved Son, and the just Lord who will not leave the tenants unpunished. He is the God of the living, to whom Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are alive, and the God who exalts His rejected Son to His right hand.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 5 is the song of the vineyard behind the parable. Psalm 118 gives the rejected stone made cornerstone. Exodus 3 is the burning bush Jesus cites for the resurrection. Psalm 110 is David calling the Christ his Lord.
basic.oneStory: In His last days of temple teaching, Jesus meets every challenge as the rightful Lord, unveiling Himself as the beloved Son the builders reject and God vindicates as cornerstone. The story drives toward the cross, where the tenants will kill the Son, and toward the resurrection and exaltation by which God makes Him the foundation and the Lord.

context.literary: This chapter belongs to the temple-teaching days of the passion week, a running battle of controversy stories that display Jesus' wisdom and the leaders' hostility. The parable of the tenants is the hinge, interpreting the whole conflict. Each question, taxes, resurrection, David's son, ends with Jesus victorious and His opponents silenced or condemned.
context.historical: The demand for authority reflects the leaders' role as guardians of the temple, now cleansed by Jesus. Tenant farming and absentee landlords were common, and killing an heir to claim land was a real if extreme scenario. The tax question was a political trap: to endorse the poll tax risked the crowd, to reject it risked Rome. The Sadducees accepted only the five books of Moses, which is why Jesus argues from Exodus.
context.canonical: Isaiah's vineyard, Psalm 118's stone, and Psalm 110's Lord are woven together to identify Jesus as the rejected yet exalted Messiah, a cluster the apostles later preach in Acts and the epistles. The resurrection argument grounds the hope of the age to come, developed fully in 1 Corinthians 15. The warning against the scribes echoes the prophets' concern for widows and the poor.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal Jesus' true authority against the leaders' challenge, to expose them as the murderous tenants who reject the beloved Son, and to display His wisdom and lordship over the state, over death, and over David himself, while warning against the hypocrisy of the scribes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The controversies narrate real events, but they carry abiding teaching: submit to the authority of the Son, render to God and to government their proper due, trust the God of the living for the resurrection, and confess the Christ as Lord. The warning against the scribes prescribes humility and care for the vulnerable.

debates.question: What does 'give to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's' establish?

views.label: A real but limited authority for the state under God
views.case: The coin bears Caesar's image, so it may be rendered to him; ordinary civic obligations like taxes are legitimate. Yet the second clause is the weightier: human beings bear God's image and owe Him everything, so Caesar's claims are real but bounded by the total claim of God.

views.label: A shrewd escape that reframes the trap
views.case: Jesus neither endorses nor condemns Roman rule directly but exposes the questioners' own compromise, they carry Caesar's coin, and lifts the issue to a higher plane. The saying refuses their political trap while asserting God's supreme claim over all of life.

debates.bearing: Both readings uphold that the state has a genuine but limited authority, while God, whose image we bear, claims the whole of our lives, so the deeper summons is to render ourselves wholly to Him.

debates.question: How does Jesus prove the resurrection from Moses?

views.label: From God's covenant name to the living patriarchs
views.case: At the burning bush, long after their deaths, God says 'I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob'. Since He is the God of the living, not the dead, the patriarchs must live to Him and will be raised; the covenant bond God keeps with them cannot be broken by death.

views.label: From the character of God's covenant faithfulness
views.case: The argument turns on who God is: a God who binds Himself to His people in covenant love will not abandon them to the grave. The resurrection is the necessary outworking of His faithfulness, so to deny it is to misjudge God Himself, as Jesus says the Sadducees do.

debates.bearing: Either way the resurrection rests not on human speculation but on the character and covenant of the living God, to whom all His people are alive, a certainty embodied in the risen Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The leaders demand to know Jesus' exousia, His authority, the right to teach and to cleanse the temple, yet they will not acknowledge the authority of heaven standing before them, and He exposes their evasion over John. In the parable He applies to Himself Psalm 118: He is the kephale gonias, the head of the corner, the stone the builders rejected which God makes the cornerstone, so that their rejection becomes, by God's reversal, His exaltation. Against the Sadducees He defends the anastasis, the resurrection, arguing from Moses that God, who calls Himself the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, is the God of the living, so the patriarchs will rise. And from Psalm 110 He shows that David calls the Christ his kyrios, his Lord, so the Messiah is not merely David's descendant but David's exalted Lord at God's right hand. Every title points to who Jesus truly is.

typology.type: The vineyard of Israel and its faithless keepers, Isaiah 5:1-7
typology.fulfillment: The vineyard let to wicked tenants who kill the beloved son, the leaders who reject Christ, Luke 20:9-16.

typology.type: The rejected stone made the cornerstone, Psalm 118:22
typology.fulfillment: Christ rejected by the builders and made the foundation by God, Luke 20:17.

typology.type: The God of the living patriarchs at the burning bush, Exodus 3:6
typology.fulfillment: The guarantee of the resurrection, since God is the God of the living, Luke 20:37-38.

study.christ: Jesus stands in the temple as the One whose authority the leaders cannot place because they will not bow to it. In the parable He unveils Himself as the beloved Son, sent last after the prophets and about to be thrown out of the vineyard and killed, yet He is also the rejected stone that God will make the cornerstone, so that the cross gives way to exaltation. He is Lord over Caesar's coin and over the God to whom the image of God in us belongs. He is the Lord of the resurrection, proving from Moses that the God of the living will raise His people, a resurrection He will Himself pioneer. And He is David's Lord, seated at God's right hand, the Christ who is greater than David could imagine.
study.god: The God of this chapter is the patient owner of the vineyard who sends messenger after messenger and finally His own beloved Son, bearing long with rebellion yet not forever. He is just, and will hold the murderous tenants to account. He is the God who reverses human verdicts, taking the stone the builders reject and making it the cornerstone of His house. He is the God of the living, faithful to His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob beyond the grave, so that resurrection is the sure outworking of His faithfulness. And He is the Father who exalts His rejected Son to His right hand until every enemy is made a footstool.

crossReferences.note: The song of the vineyard God planted, the backdrop to the parable of the wicked tenants.
The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone, applied by Jesus to Himself.
God at the burning bush names Himself the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, Jesus' proof of resurrection.
The LORD said to my Lord, sit at My right hand, showing the Christ is David's Lord.

study.oneStory: In His last temple days Jesus meets every trap as the rightful Lord, and in the parable of the tenants He tells His own story in advance: the beloved Son rejected and killed, yet made by God the cornerstone of a new house. His answers reveal Him as Lord over the state, over death, and over David himself. The narrative presses toward the cross, where the tenants will do their worst, and toward the resurrection and exaltation by which God overturns their verdict and seats His Son at His right hand.

quiz.q: How does Jesus respond to the demand to know His authority?

options.text: He asks whether John's baptism was from heaven or men, trapping His challengers and declining to answer them.
options.note: Luke 20:3-8 shows the counter-question that exposes their bad faith.

options.text: He turns the question back on them with a question of His own.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific question about John and its exposing effect.

options.text: He explains in detail that His authority comes from God.
options.note: He does not spell it out; He exposes their evasion instead.

options.text: He admits He has no authority to act as He does.
options.note: He never concedes this; He implies a heavenly authority they will not acknowledge.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the wicked tenants reveal?

options.text: The leaders reject the beloved Son as the tenants kill the heir, but God makes the rejected stone the cornerstone.
options.note: Luke 20:9-17 casts the leaders as tenants and Jesus as the rejected, exalted stone.

options.text: The tenants killed the owner's son to seize the inheritance.
options.note: True to the story, but it omits the identification of Jesus as the rejected cornerstone.

options.text: God is patient with His vineyard for a long time.
options.note: His patience is shown, but the point is the rejection of the Son and God's vindication of Him.

options.text: The tenants were justified in keeping the vineyard.
options.note: They are condemned; the owner will destroy them and give the vineyard to others.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by 'give to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's'?

options.text: The state has a real but limited claim, while God, whose image we bear, claims all of life.
options.note: Luke 20:24-25 grants Caesar his coin yet asserts God's total claim.

options.text: It is lawful to pay taxes to Caesar.
options.note: True, but it omits the weightier claim of God over the whole of our lives.

options.text: Religion and government must never touch each other.
options.note: Jesus distinguishes their claims but subordinates all to God, not a strict separation.

options.text: We owe Caesar nothing at all.
options.note: He affirms that Caesar's due should be rendered to him.

quiz.q: How does Jesus prove the resurrection to the Sadducees?

options.text: From Moses: God calls Himself the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and He is the God of the living.
options.note: Luke 20:37-38 argues from Exodus that the patriarchs live to God.

options.text: He shows that God keeps His covenant beyond death.
options.note: True in substance, but it omits the specific proof from the burning bush that they accepted.

options.text: He appeals to the prophets' promises of new life.
options.note: He argues from Moses, whom the Sadducees accepted, not from the prophets.

options.text: He admits the resurrection cannot be proven from Scripture.
options.note: The opposite: He proves it directly from Moses.

quiz.q: What point does Jesus make about the Christ and David?

options.text: David calls the Christ his Lord in Psalm 110, so the Messiah is more than David's son; He is David's Lord.
options.note: Luke 20:41-44 shows the Christ is David's Lord, pointing to His divine exaltation.

options.text: The Christ is descended from David.
options.note: True and assumed, but Jesus' point is that He is also David's Lord, greater than a mere son.

options.text: David wrote many psalms about the Messiah.
options.note: The specific point is the title 'Lord' in Psalm 110, not David's authorship in general.

options.text: The Christ has nothing to do with David.
options.note: Jesus affirms the Christ is David's son, while showing He is also David's Lord.

questions: The leaders demanded Jesus' credentials but would not submit to His authority. Where do you question God's right to rule a part of your life you would rather keep for yourself?
The tenants wanted the vineyard without the owner and killed the Son to get it. In what ways are you tempted to want God's gifts while resisting His claim on you?
Jesus said to render to God what bears His image, and you bear it. What would it mean to give God not just a slice of your life but the whole of it?
The Sadducees could not imagine resurrection because they underestimated God. Where has a small view of God shrunk your hope, and how does the God of the living enlarge it?
Jesus warned against religion done for show while widows were devoured. Where might your outward devotion be masking neglect of the vulnerable or a love of being seen?

translationNotes.note: 'John's baptism, was it from heaven, or from men?' renders a clause set off in the printed text with an em dash, here a comma. The question is a trap that exposes the leaders' unwillingness to judge honestly.

Jesus quotes Psalm 118:22, the stone the builders rejected becoming the cornerstone, applying the Scripture to His own rejection and vindication. The image becomes central to the apostles' preaching in Acts and the epistles.

Verse 30 is very brief, 'Then the second', because some later manuscripts expand it with a repeated clause about the brother marrying and dying. The BSB follows the shorter earliest text; the meaning of the Sadducees' riddle is unaffected.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 20:1-47. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, poem markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dash in verse 4 was converted to a comma. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
